title: Consistency-Driven Adaptive Depth for Diffusion Transformers
description: We propose CAD-DiT, a training-free acceleration framework for Diffusion Transformers that enables per-token adaptive depth using prediction consistency as a convergence signal. Unlike existing training-free methods that apply uniform strategies (DeepCache, Δ-DiT) or learned methods that require fine-tuning (DiffRatio-MoD), CAD-DiT allows different spatial positions to use different transformer depths within the same denoising step based on a training-free consistency metric measuring prediction stability across consecutive layers.
motivation: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) are computationally expensive, but existing training-free acceleration methods treat all spatial positions uniformly, missing opportunities for content-dependent optimization. Meanwhile, training-based methods like DiffRatio-MoD achieve spatial adaptation through learned routers but require expensive fine-tuning. There is a critical gap: no training-free method exists for per-token adaptive depth allocation in DiTs. We observe that different image regions converge at different rates within transformer layers—simple backgrounds stabilize quickly while complex textures require deeper processing—and the model's own prediction consistency can serve as a natural, training-free signal for convergence.
proposed_approach: CAD-DiT introduces three components: (1) Prediction Consistency Metric (PCM): a training-free measure of token-level convergence based on prediction stability across consecutive layers—computed as the normalized difference between predictions at layer l and l-1; (2) Token-Conditional Forward Pass: a modified inference loop where each token conditionally continues to deeper layers or exits based on its PCM score, with exited tokens excluded from subsequent computation; (3) Timestep-Aware Thresholding: dynamic consistency thresholds that are lower (more aggressive) in early timesteps and higher (more conservative) in late timesteps for detail refinement. The method requires no learned routers, no exit heads, and no fine-tuning.
hypothesis: A training-free consistency metric measuring prediction stability across consecutive transformer layers can serve as a reliable signal for per-token adaptive depth in Diffusion Transformers, enabling spatially-heterogeneous computation that reduces per-step FLOPs by 30-50% while maintaining generation quality.

confirmed: CAD-DiT achieves ≥30% per-step FLOP reduction with FID increase <3 points on ImageNet 256×256
Spatial patterns show meaningful variation (e.g., background tokens exit earlier than foreground)
Method works across multiple DiT architectures without any retraining or fine-tuning
Qualitative visual quality is comparable to full model
refuted: FID degradation exceeds 5 points at 30% FLOP reduction
Prediction consistency is not correlated with actual generation quality
Overhead of consistency computation outweighs the savings
Spatially-varying depth provides no benefit over simple global thresholds

key_differentiators: First training-free method for per-token adaptive depth in DiTs—no learned routers, no exit heads, no fine-tuning
Uses only signals already present in model's forward pass (prediction consistency)
Content-dependent adaptation: different tokens use different depths based on actual convergence behavior
Positioned as practical alternative to DiffRatio-MoD when fine-tuning is infeasible

related_work.paper: DiffRatio-MoD: Layer- and Timestep-Adaptive Differentiable Token Compression Ratios for Efficient Diffusion Transformers
related_work.difference: DiffRatio-MoD uses learned routers requiring joint fine-tuning. CAD-DiT achieves similar spatial adaptation through training-free consistency metrics, making it applicable without any training.

related_work.paper: DeepCache: Accelerating Diffusion Models for Free
related_work.difference: DeepCache reuses features across timesteps but uses full model depth per timestep. CAD-DiT reduces per-timestep computation through adaptive depth.

related_work.paper: Δ-DiT: A Training-Free Acceleration Method Tailored for Diffusion Transformers
related_work.difference: Δ-DiT uses fixed schedules for layer skipping, applying same depth to all tokens. CAD-DiT adapts depth per-token based on content.

related_work.paper: DeeDiff: Dynamic Uncertainty-Aware Early Exiting for Accelerating Diffusion Model Generation
related_work.difference: DeeDiff makes global early exit decisions—all tokens use same depth. CAD-DiT enables spatially-heterogeneous exit depths.
ASE uses predefined timestep schedules for block skipping. CAD-DiT is input-dependent with per-token decisions.

related_work.paper: Just on Time: Token-Level Early Stopping for Diffusion Language Models
related_work.difference: Jot applies token-level early stopping to diffusion language models across denoising steps. CAD-DiT applies consistency-based adaptive depth to image generation DiTs within transformer layers.

expected_contributions: First demonstration of training-free per-token adaptive depth for DiT acceleration
Prediction Consistency Metric: a training-free signal for token-level convergence in diffusion models
Empirical analysis of spatial convergence patterns in DiT generation
Practical acceleration method applicable without training infrastructure
feasibility: The proposed method is feasible within the given constraints (1x A6000, 8 hours): (1) It is truly training-free—only inference-time modifications with no additional parameters; (2) DiT-XL/2 can run on a single A6000 with 48GB VRAM; (3) The consistency metric adds minimal overhead (vector norm computation); (4) Experiments on ImageNet validation subset (~10K samples) for FID computation is standard practice; (5) Core experiments can be completed in 6-8 hours.

positioning_vs_diff_ratio_mod.acknowledgment: DiffRatio-MoD (CVPR 2025) is concurrent/complementary work that achieves spatial adaptation through learned routers with fine-tuning.
positioning_vs_diff_ratio_mod.key_difference: CAD-DiT provides training-free spatial adaptation; DiffRatio-MoD requires fine-tuning.
positioning_vs_diff_ratio_mod.use_cases_cad_dit: Applicable when fine-tuning is infeasible: proprietary models, limited training data, no training infrastructure
positioning_vs_diff_ratio_mod.use_cases_diff_ratio: Better when maximum quality-efficiency trade-off is needed and fine-tuning is possible
positioning_vs_diff_ratio_mod.relationship: CAD-DiT is a practical alternative, not a replacement—positioned for different deployment scenarios
